notes: Cray commercial customers accounted for 50% of all Cray sales revenue by 1995
10% of Cray's overall business came from databases and database applications (OLTP and DSS)
CS6400 supports up to 5 Terabytes of online storage with expandability beyond via disk arrays
CS6400 scales from 4 to 64 Sun SPARC-based CPUs in SMP configuration
Internal system bandwidth of 1.76 GB/s enabling high data throughput
Physical memory configurable up to 16 Gigabytes
Binary compatibility with Sun SPARC enables access to 10000+ ISV applications
Cray targeting 20–500 GB database range with sub-second response time promise
Cray formed Business Systems Division (BSD) specifically to pursue commercial database market
BSD formed by merging Cray Solutions (consulting) and Cray Research Superservers
Aberdeen predicts Cray will be 'a major force in the high end of the commercial database marketplace'
Cray Research acquired by Silicon Graphics (SGI) for $740M in February 1996; CS6400 Superservers divested to Sun in May 1996
Cray posted loss of $226.4M on revenue of $676.2M in fiscal 1995, revenue down 27% YoY
IBM SP2 cited as primary high-end Unix database competitor to Cray CS6400
AT&T GIS 3600 listed as competitor in high-end database segment
nCUBE 2/3 listed as MPP-based competitor for high-end data workloads
Aberdeen implies SMP architecture (CS6400) is the right platform for commercial database growth
Sun acquired CS6400 Superservers from SGI in 1996; evolved into Sun Enterprise 10000 (Starfire) which was highly successful
CS6400 supports up to 64 I/O channels
Cray controls approximately 70% of supercomputer market for systems costing more than $5M
Cray-enhanced Solaris enables system managers to dedicate processor groups to specific tasks
Hot-swap capability allows admins to deallocate power and replace/reassign components without downtime
Newly named CEO J. Phillip Samper previously revitalized Sun Microsystems Computer Corporation
BSD headed by Bobbi Hazard, VP/GM reporting to President Robert H. Ewald
Cray T3D MPP system retained for scientific/engineering workloads requiring >5TB
Aberdeen presents Cray as viable independent commercial DB vendor; contradicted by SGI acquisition just months later
SGI sold Cray supercomputer division to Tera Computer Company in March 2000; renamed Cray Inc.; still active today
Aberdeen explicitly recommends IS decision makers considering IBM SP2 should compare Cray's focused open product offering
